summary: Yegge argues that XEmacs, despite its historical importance in pushing Emacs forward with GUI features and innovation, should be retired in favor of GNU Emacs. He presents evidence that XEmacs suffers from chronic instability, poor performance, more bugs, and API incompatibilities that fragment the developer community. The broader argument is that Emacs itself faces existential threats from browsers like Firefox, which combine extensibility with modern rendering capabilities. To compete, the Emacs community must unify behind GNU Emacs and focus on critical improvements: better rendering, multi-language VM support, and concurrency.
key_points: XEmacs historically drove Emacs innovation but now lags behind GNU Emacs in stability, performance, and features
API incompatibilities between XEmacs and GNU Emacs waste developer effort and fragment the community
Emacs is uniquely 'self-hosting' - improvements to the environment compound because you're enhancing the tools you use to make enhancements
The real threat to Emacs isn't IDEs but browsers, which combine extensibility with modern rendering and could eventually eclipse Emacs
Emacs needs three major evolutions to survive: better rendering engine, multi-language VM support, and concurrency
money_quotes: XEmacs has never been a particularly good tool for serious Emacs users because even though it's written in C, it crashes like a mature C++ application.
Emacs is self-hosting: writing things in it makes the environment itself more powerful. It's a feedback loop: a recursive, self-reinforcing, multiplicative effect.

Writing an Eclipse or IntelliJ plugin is a monumental effort, so almost nobody does it. This means there's no community of building and customizing your own tools, which has long been the hallmark of great programmers.
If Emacs can't find a way to evolve into (or merge with) Firefox, then Firefox or some other extensible browser is going to eclipse Emacs.

I suspect that in self-hosted environments, you can eventually cross a threshold where your performance gains from features implemented in the hosted environment outpace the gains from features in the substrate.
Tabs don't scale. Tabbed browsing was revolutionary in the same way adding more tellers to a bank was revolutionary: it's, like, 4x better.
Dynamic typing wins again, as it always will.
key_insight: Software communities must recognize when competition has served its purpose and unification becomes necessary for survival against external threats.
